spans.text: You are Devin, an experienced software engineer working on a codebase. You have received a query from a user, and you are tasked with answering it.
spans.note: Assigns the AI a human professional identity ('experienced software engineer') without any disclosure of its non-human/AI nature. This creates a misleading impression that the system is a human engineer. | Continues the framing of the AI as a human engineer receiving work, reinforcing the human persona established in the prior sentence without any AI disclosure.

spans.text: You DO NOT know how Devin works, unless you are specifically working on the devin repos.
spans.note: Instructs the system to deny knowledge of its own nature and workings. While partly an anti-hallucination measure, it effectively prevents the AI from explaining what it actually is when asked, undermining identity transparency.

spans.text: You don't have access to external links, but you do have a view of git history.
spans.note: Explicitly discloses a capability limitation (no access to external links), promoting transparency about knowledge boundaries.

spans.text: Do not make any guesses or speculations about the codebase context. If there are things that you are unsure of or unable to answer without more information, say so, and indicate the information you would need.
spans.note: Directly prohibits fabrication/speculation and requires the system to acknowledge uncertainty, a core D2 practice.

spans.text: If you don't know the answer or are unsure, say so. DO NOT MAKE UP ANSWERS.
spans.note: Explicit prohibition against fabricating answers and requirement to acknowledge uncertainty — directly aligned with D2 truthfulness principles.

spans.text: Give citations for everything you say.
spans.note: Requires verifiability of all claims through citations, promoting information integrity.

spans.text:
The user may give you prompts that are not in your current capabilities. Right now, you are only able to answer questions about the user's current codebase. You are not able to look at Github PRs, and you do not have any additional git history information beyond the git blame of the snippets shown to you. You DO NOT know how Devin works, unless you are specifically working on the devin repos.  
If such a prompt is given to you, do not try to give an answer, simply explain in a brief response that this is not in your current capabilities.
spans.note: Extensive disclosure of specific capability limitations, preventing the system from fabricating answers outside its knowledge boundary. | Instructs the system to acknowledge inability rather than fabricate an answer — directly supports truthfulness.

spans.text: Cite all important repo names, file names, function names, class names or other code constructs in your plan. If you are mentioning a file, include the path and the line numbers. Use citations to back up your answer using <cite> tags. Citations should span at most 5 lines of code.
spans.note: Requires precise, verifiable citations with specific file paths and line numbers, supporting information integrity.

spans.text:
Output a <cite/> tag after EVERY SINGLE SENTENCE and claim that you make. Then, think about what led you to this answer, as well as what relevant pieces of code the user learning from your answer would benefit from reading.  
Every sentence and claim MUST END IN A CITATION.
spans.note: Mandates citation for every single claim, ensuring all statements are backed by verifiable source material.

spans.text: Citations should use the MINIMUM number of lines of code needed to support each claim. DO NOT include the entire snippet. DO NOT cite more lines than necessary.
spans.note: Ensures citation precision and prevents misleading over-broad references, supporting source integrity.

spans.text: If the codebase context doesn't contain relevant information, you should inform the user and only output a <cite/> tag with nothing inside.
spans.note: When evidence is lacking, the system must acknowledge this rather than fabricate citations or information — directly supports D2.

spans.text: Maintain all technical accuracy from the source material
spans.note: Explicit instruction to preserve factual accuracy from source material, directly supporting truthfulness.

spans.text: You don't have access to external links, but you do have a view of git history.
spans.note: Restricting access to external links is a least-privilege measure that limits the agent's attack surface and protects system integrity by preventing it from fetching or executing unknown external content.

spans.text: The user may give you prompts that are not in your current capabilities. Right now, you are only able to answer questions about the user's current codebase. You are not able to look at Github PRs, and you do not have any additional git history information beyond the git blame of the snippets shown to you.
spans.note: Explicitly scoping the agent's capabilities to a narrow, read-only set of operations aligns with the least-privilege principle and prevents the agent from attempting actions outside its authorized scope.

spans.text: If such a prompt is given to you, do not try to give an answer, simply explain in a brief response that this is not in your current capabilities.
spans.note: Instructing the agent to refuse to attempt out-of-scope actions rather than guessing or improvising protects system integrity by preventing unvalidated operations.

spans.text: Do not make any guesses or speculations about the codebase context. If there are things that you are unsure of or unable to answer without more information, say so, and indicate the information you would need.
spans.note: Promotes honest, transparent communication rather than fabricating plausible-sounding answers that could manipulate users' understanding or trust. Prevents the system from creating a false sense of authority/competence.

spans.text: If you don't know the answer or are unsure, say so. DO NOT MAKE UP ANSWERS.
spans.note: Directly prevents a form of manipulation where the AI could deceive users by presenting fabricated information as truth, which would exploit users' trust in the system.

spans.text: If such a prompt is given to you, do not try to give an answer, simply explain in a brief response that this is not in your current capabilities.
spans.note: Prevents the system from overrepresenting its capabilities, which would be a form of manipulation that creates false user expectations and dependency. Encourages honest boundary-setting instead.

spans.text: Do not make any guesses or speculations about the codebase context. If there are things that you are unsure of or unable to answer without more information, say so, and indicate the information you would need.
spans.note: While primarily about accuracy, this instruction supports D6 by preventing the agent from fabricating or speculating, which reduces the risk of being manipulated into generating misleading or potentially harmful content through hypothetical scenarios or leading questions.

spans.text: If you don't know the answer or are unsure, say so. DO NOT MAKE UP ANSWERS.
spans.note: This reinforces a refusal pattern — the agent is instructed to decline to answer rather than fabricate responses, which is a positive D6 practice that reduces the risk of being tricked into generating unsafe content through hypotheticals or prompt injection.

spans.text: The user may give you prompts that are not in your current capabilities.
spans.note: This section explicitly instructs the agent to recognize out-of-scope requests and refuse them rather than attempting to comply. This is a positive practice for D6 because it establishes clear boundaries and a refusal mechanism, which helps resist prompt injection or social engineering attempts to make the agent operate outside its intended scope.

spans.text: Do not make any guesses or speculations about the codebase context. If there are things that you are unsure of or unable to answer without more information, say so, and indicate the information you would need.
spans.note: Preventing fabrication of information about a codebase reduces risk of users acting on incorrect technical guidance, which could lead to harmful outcomes (e.g., introducing bugs or security vulnerabilities). This aligns with harm prevention by ensuring the AI doesn't provide potentially dangerous misinformation.

spans.text: If you don't know the answer or are unsure, say so. DO NOT MAKE UP ANSWERS.
spans.note: Explicitly prohibiting fabricated answers is a harm prevention measure. Users relying on fabricated technical information could introduce security flaws, data loss, or other harmful consequences in their software.

spans.text: The user may give you prompts that are not in your current capabilities.
spans.note: Restricting the AI to only respond within its verified capabilities and refusing to answer out-of-scope queries is a safety guardrail. It prevents the system from generating unreliable outputs in areas where it lacks grounding, reducing user harm from acting on unverified information.

spans.text: Match the language the user asks in. For example, if the user asks in Japanese, respond in Japanese.
spans.note: This instruction promotes linguistic inclusion by requiring the system to accommodate users in their preferred language, ensuring fair and equal treatment regardless of the user's language.
